subject: family_law
topic_heading: Federal Child Support Guidelines — Table Amount
subtopic_heading: Table Amount — Payor Income and Number of Children
fact_pattern: Daniel and Fiona separated when their son Marcus was 16. Marcus is now 20 years old and enrolled full-time at university, living in residence during the school year and returning to Fiona's home during breaks. Marcus has moderate scholarship income of $4,000/year and no other means of support. Daniel earns $90,000/year gross income in Ontario. He argues that because Marcus is over 18, the FCSG table amount automatically applies as the correct amount. Fiona argues the court should depart from the table amount given Marcus's circumstances.
call_of_question: Under the Federal Child Support Guidelines, which provision governs child support for Marcus, and does the FCSG table amount automatically apply?

options.A: Once a child turns 18, child support obligations terminate automatically; no support order can be made for an adult child.
options.B: The FCSG table amount applies automatically to all children, regardless of age, as long as they are enrolled in post-secondary education.
options.C: Under FCSG s.3(2)(b), where a child is the age of majority or over and child support is sought, the court must determine the amount in accordance with the table unless, given the child's condition, means, needs, or other circumstances, and the financial ability of each spouse to contribute, the table amount would be inappropriate; the court retains discretion to award more or less than the table amount for adult children.
options.D: For adult children in post-secondary education, the court has unlimited discretion to set any amount it considers fair without reference to the FCSG table.

explanation: Under FCSG s.3(2)(b), where a child has reached the age of majority or is over the age of majority, child support is governed differently from the s.3(1) mandatory table rule. The court must determine support in accordance with the table unless — having regard to the child's condition, means, needs, or other circumstances, and the financial ability of each spouse to contribute — the table amount would be inappropriate. Marcus's full-time post-secondary enrollment, modest scholarship income, and dependence on Fiona's household are exactly the circumstances s.3(2)(b) requires the court to weigh. The table amount is not automatically applied; it is the starting point subject to a context-specific appropriateness assessment. The court may award the table amount, a different amount, or apportion costs of education between the parents under the s.3(2)(b) analysis.
why_A_wrong: Exception Trap. Child support obligations do not terminate automatically at age 18. Under Divorce Act s.2(1), a 'child of the marriage' includes an adult child who is unable, by reason of illness, disability, or other cause, to withdraw from parental charge — which courts have consistently held to include full-time post-secondary education.
why_B_wrong: General Rule Trap. FCSG s.3(1) (table amount applies automatically) governs children under majority age. Once a child reaches majority, s.3(2)(b) introduces an appropriateness assessment, meaning the table is not automatically applied but is subject to review of the child's and parents' circumstances.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.3(2)(b) applies to adult children and requires the court to consider whether the table amount is appropriate given the adult child's condition, means, needs, and circumstances and each parent's financial ability to contribute.
why_D_wrong: Overstatement Trap. FCSG s.3(2)(b) does not give courts unlimited discretion to ignore the table entirely. The table amount remains the reference point; the court departs from it only if the table amount would be inappropriate given the specific s.3(2)(b) factors.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Federal Child Support Guidelines — Table Amount
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 3(2)(b); Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 2(1)

tested_concepts: child of the marriage over majority
discretion to depart from table for adult children

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Federal Child Support Guidelines — Table Amount
subtopic_heading: Determination of Income for FCSG Purposes
fact_pattern: Victor is self-employed operating a consulting business. His corporation reports pre-tax income of $200,000, but Victor pays himself a salary of $80,000 and reinvests the remaining $120,000 in the corporation. He argues that child support should be based only on his $80,000 salary. His former spouse argues that the court should look beyond the corporate salary.
call_of_question: How is Victor's income determined for purposes of the Federal Child Support Guidelines when he is a self-employed shareholder of a private corporation?

options.A: Victor's income is his declared salary of $80,000; the court cannot look behind the corporate structure.
options.B: Victor's income is the pre-tax corporate income of $200,000 because the Guidelines always attribute the entire corporate income to the shareholder-payor.
options.C: Under FCSG Schedule III and s.18, where a payor controls a private corporation, the court may include in income amounts that the corporation earned but did not distribute as salary, where those amounts are not required for business operations and the retention constitutes an artificial reduction of the payor's income.
options.D: Victor's income is determined by averaging his salary over the last five years, ignoring corporate profits entirely.

explanation: Under Federal Child Support Guidelines s.18(1), where a paying spouse controls a corporation, the court may determine the spouse's annual income to include the pre-tax income of the corporation, less any amounts that are reasonably required for the operation of the business. This prevents artificial suppression of income by retaining earnings in a corporation. The court does not automatically attribute all corporate income, but will look behind the salary when the retention of earnings is not required for business purposes and results in an artificial understatement of the payor's income.
why_A_wrong: Courts regularly look behind corporate structures when a payor controls the corporation. FCSG s.18 specifically addresses this situation and permits the court to include undistributed corporate income in the payor's income for Guidelines purposes.
why_B_wrong: The court does not automatically attribute the entire pre-tax corporate income to the payor. Under s.18, only amounts not reasonably required for business operations may be added back. Amounts genuinely reinvested for business purposes may be excluded.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.18 allows the court to include undistributed corporate income where the payor controls the corporation and the retention is not required for legitimate business purposes.
why_D_wrong: Income averaging over five years may be used in appropriate circumstances under FCSG Schedule III, but it does not eliminate the court's ability to consider corporate income under s.18. The five-year averaging approach is not the default rule for self-employed payors with private corporations.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Determination of Income — FCSG s.18 and Schedule III
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 18(1), Schedule III

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Special and Extraordinary Expenses — FCSG s.7
subtopic_heading: Categories of Special and Extraordinary Expenses Under FCSG s.7
fact_pattern: Olivia and Steve have one child, aged 7, who lives primarily with Olivia. Steve pays the table amount of child support. Olivia now asks the court to order Steve to contribute to the following additional expenses: (1) daycare costs of $1,200/month while Olivia works; (2) the child's hockey equipment and registration fees of $3,000/year; (3) private school tuition of $15,000/year. Steve argues none of these are s.7 expenses.
call_of_question: Which of these three items is/are properly characterized as special or extraordinary expenses under FCSG s.7?

options.A: None — s.7 expenses are limited to medical and dental costs; daycare, sports, and school tuition are not covered.
options.B: Daycare costs and private school tuition are s.7 expenses; recreational sports expenses are not s.7 expenses under any circumstances.
options.C: All three may qualify as s.7 expenses: daycare (s.7(1)(a) — child care required by employment), private school tuition (s.7(1)(d) — education programs necessary for the child or exceeding what is reasonably expected at the public school level); hockey may qualify as an extraordinary expense under s.7(1)(f) if it is an 'extraordinary' extracurricular activity given the parties' circumstances.
options.D: Only private school tuition qualifies because s.7 is limited to educational expenses above the public school level.

explanation: Under the Federal Child Support Guidelines s.7(1) lists categories of special or extraordinary expenses: (a) child care required by employment/illness/disability; (b) medical/dental insurance premiums; (c) medical/dental expenses exceeding insurance; (d) education programs (including post-secondary and programs necessary for the child's needs); (e) post-secondary education expenses; (f) extraordinary extracurricular activities. Child care while Olivia works is clearly s.7(1)(a). Private school tuition may qualify under s.7(1)(d). Hockey registration may qualify as an extraordinary extracurricular activity under s.7(1)(f), assessed in the context of the parties' incomes and historical activities. All three potentially qualify; the court applies a proportionality test under s.7(2) based on income.
why_A_wrong: Section 7 is not limited to medical and dental costs. It expressly covers child care (s.7(1)(a)), educational programs (s.7(1)(d)), and extraordinary extracurricular activities (s.7(1)(f)), among other categories.
why_B_wrong: Extracurricular activities can qualify as extraordinary s.7 expenses under s.7(1)(f) where they are extraordinary given the family's means and lifestyle. It is incorrect to categorically exclude sports from s.7.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. All three items potentially qualify under specific s.7 subsections, and each is allocated between the parties in proportion to their incomes under s.7(2).
why_D_wrong: Section 7 covers much more than education above the public school level. It includes child care, medical expenses, and extracurricular activities, among other categories.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Special and Extraordinary Expenses — FCSG s.7
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 7(1)(a), s. 7(1)(d), s. 7(1)(f), s. 7(2)

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Special and Extraordinary Expenses — FCSG s.7
subtopic_heading: Apportionment of Section 7 Expenses Between Parents
fact_pattern: Diane earns $120,000 per year and her former husband Carl earns $80,000 per year. Their daughter participates in competitive gymnastics with annual costs of $6,000 and receives a $600 athletic bursary from her school board applied directly toward gymnastics. The court has determined the gymnastics cost qualifies as an extraordinary extracurricular expense under FCSG s.7(1)(f). Carl acknowledges s.7 applies but argues he should pay the same dollar amount as Diane since both are parents.
call_of_question: What amount does Carl owe under FCSG s.7(2) after accounting for the bursary, given the parties' respective incomes?

options.A: Carl pays $2,400 — half of the net $4,800 expense after deducting the bursary, because parents split s.7 expenses equally.
options.B: Carl pays $3,000 — half of the gross $6,000 expense; the bursary belongs to the child and cannot reduce either parent's obligation.
options.C: Carl pays $2,160 — under FCSG s.7(2), the bursary of $600 is first deducted from the $6,000 gross cost leaving $5,400 net, which is then apportioned 60/40 in proportion to the parties' incomes; Carl's 40% share of $5,400 is $2,160.
options.D: Diane pays the full $5,400 net expense because she earns more than Carl; Carl owes nothing.

explanation: Under the Federal Child Support Guidelines s.7(2) provides that s.7 expenses are apportioned in proportion to each parent's income after deducting any contribution from the child. Step 1: Deduct the child's bursary — $6,000 minus $600 = $5,400 net expense. Step 2: Determine income proportions — Diane's $120,000 is 60% of the combined $200,000; Carl's $80,000 is 40%. Step 3: Apply proportions — Diane pays $3,240 (60%); Carl pays $2,160 (40%). The s.7(2) deduction of the child's contribution before apportionment is mandatory. The 50/50 split (option A) misapplies the proportionality rule. Ignoring the bursary deduction (option B) skips the mandatory first step. The higher earner bearing all costs (option D) misreads 'proportionate' as 'full obligation.'
why_A_wrong: General Rule Trap. Section 7 expenses are not split equally. FCSG s.7(2) requires proportionate contribution based on each parent's income — here 60:40. An equal split would only arise where incomes happen to be identical. Moreover, the bursary must be deducted first.
why_B_wrong: Wrong Timeline / Sequencing Trap. The bursary is a contribution from the child that must be deducted from the gross cost before apportionment, as required by s.7(2). Ignoring this deduction overstates the net expense available for apportionment between the parents.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.7(2) requires deducting the child's $600 bursary first ($5,400 net), then apportioning 60/40 by income; Carl's 40% share is $2,160.
why_D_wrong: Overstatement Trap. The higher-earning parent does not bear sole responsibility for s.7 expenses. Both parents contribute proportionally. Diane bearing all costs would contradict the proportionate contribution scheme in s.7(2).

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Section 7 Expenses — Apportionment and Child Contribution
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 7(2)

tested_concepts: FCSG s.7(2) child contribution deducted before apportionment

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Imputing Income — Purposeful Underemployment and Related Circumstances
fact_pattern: Brian, who has a law degree and five years of litigation experience, voluntarily resigned from his position as an associate earning $130,000/year the day before a child support motion. He claims he is now self-employed doing occasional legal consulting, earning $20,000/year. His former spouse seeks an imputation of income. Brian argues the court must accept his actual income.
call_of_question: Under FCSG s.19, in what circumstances may a court impute income to a child support payor, and would Brian's circumstances warrant imputation?

options.A: Courts may only impute income in cases of criminal fraud; resignation from employment, however suspicious the timing, cannot give rise to imputation.
options.B: Courts may impute income under s.19 where a spouse is intentionally under-employed or unemployed; however, Brian's right to choose his career path means no imputation is possible.
options.C: Under FCSG s.19(1)(a), a court may impute income where a spouse is intentionally under-employed or unemployed, unless the situation is appropriate given the circumstances (such as illness, disability, or reasonable career transition); Brian's sudden voluntary resignation to near-zero income the day before a child support motion, without legitimate justification, strongly supports imputation at a level reflecting his earning capacity.
options.D: The court must average Brian's income over the last three years and use that as his income for child support purposes; imputation is not available.

explanation: Under the Federal Child Support Guidelines s.19(1)(a) permits a court to impute income to a paying spouse where the spouse is intentionally under-employed or unemployed, unless the situation is reasonable in the circumstances (e.g., disability, illness, or a genuine and reasonable career change). Brian's voluntary resignation the day before a child support motion, combined with his legal qualifications and abrupt shift to $20,000 consulting income, are strong indicators of intentional income reduction for purposes of avoiding child support. Courts regularly impute income based on earning capacity in such circumstances.
why_A_wrong: Income imputation under FCSG s.19 is not limited to criminal fraud. Intentional underemployment — including voluntary resignation for reasons connected to avoiding child support — is a recognized and common basis for imputation.
why_B_wrong: While individuals have the right to choose their careers, the right to change employment does not insulate a payor from income imputation where the change is found to be intentional underemployment under s.19(1)(a). Courts assess the bona fides and reasonableness of the career change.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.19(1)(a) authorizes imputation where intentional underemployment exists without legitimate justification; Brian's suspicious timing and dramatic income reduction strongly warrant imputation.
why_D_wrong: Three-year income averaging (available under Schedule III) is a tool for dealing with variable income, not an alternative to imputation in cases of deliberate underemployment. Imputation under s.19 is available and appropriate here.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Imputing Income — FCSG s.19
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 19(1)(a)

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Child Support in Shared Parenting Arrangements
subtopic_heading: Shared Parenting Over 40% — Offset Method Under FCSG s.9
fact_pattern: Anna earns $100,000/year and Mark earns $60,000/year. They have one child, and pursuant to a parenting order, the child spends 45% of the time with Mark and 55% with Anna. Under the Ontario table, the table amount for Mark's income of $60,000 for one child is $572/month; for Anna's income of $100,000, it is $879/month. Both parents seek to pay the minimum child support possible.
call_of_question: How does FCSG s.9 govern child support when the child spends at least 40% of the time with each parent?

options.A: When a child spends more than 40% of the time with each parent, no child support is payable by either party; the arrangement is presumed financially neutral.
options.B: FCSG s.9 creates a mandatory formula requiring the higher-earning parent to pay the lower-earning parent the full table amount.
options.C: Under FCSG s.9, where a child spends at least 40% of the time with each parent, the court must consider the table amounts for each parent, the increased cost of the shared arrangement, and the condition, means, needs, and other circumstances of each parent; the offset method (table amount for Anna minus table amount for Mark = $879 - $572 = $307/month payable by Anna) is a common starting point, but the court may adjust up or down.
options.D: Section 9 applies only where the parenting time is exactly 50/50; at 55/45 the standard table amount applies.

explanation: Under the Federal Child Support Guidelines s.9 applies where a spouse exercises parenting time with a child for not less than 40% of the time over the course of a year. In that case, the court shall take into account: (a) the amounts set out in the applicable tables for each parent; (b) the increased costs of shared parenting; and (c) the condition, means, needs and other circumstances of each spouse and the children. The offset method — comparing the table amounts and paying the difference — is frequently used as a starting point (here, $879 - $572 = $307 payable by Anna), but it is not mandatory and the court retains discretion to depart from it (see Contino v. Leonelli-Contino, [2005] 3 SCR 217).
why_A_wrong: Section 9 does not eliminate child support obligations in shared parenting arrangements. The court must conduct a three-part analysis; child support is rarely zero even in 50/50 arrangements unless incomes are equal.
why_B_wrong: FCSG s.9 does not create a mandatory formula requiring the higher earner to pay the full table amount. The three-factor test in s.9 — including the offset method as a starting point — governs the analysis.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.9 applies whenever parenting time reaches 40% or more, and the three-factor test (table amounts, increased costs, circumstances) applies; the offset is a common starting point per Contino.
why_D_wrong: Section 9 applies at 40% or more, not only at exactly 50/50. At 45% parenting time, s.9 is engaged. The 40% threshold triggers the full s.9 analysis.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Shared Parenting — FCSG s.9
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 9; Contino v. Leonelli-Contino, [2005] 3 SCR 217

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Child Support for Adult Child in Post-Secondary Education
fact_pattern: Sophie is 21 years old and enrolled in a four-year university program. Her parents divorced when she was 10. Her father argues that his child support obligation ended when Sophie turned 18 and he is no longer required to pay. Her mother applies for an order of continued child support.
call_of_question: Under the Divorce Act and Federal Child Support Guidelines, can a child support order be made or continued for an adult child attending post-secondary education?

options.A: No — child support is available only for children under 18 in Canada; turning 18 terminates all child support obligations automatically.
options.B: Yes — under DA s.2(1), the definition of 'child of the marriage' includes a child who is 18 or older and unable by reason of illness, disability, or other cause to withdraw from parental charge; attendance at university is recognized as an 'other cause' preventing withdrawal from parental charge, and Sophie may qualify.
options.C: Yes — but only until Sophie turns 21; after age 21, child support for post-secondary students is unavailable.
options.D: Yes — but support for adult post-secondary students is capped at $1,000/month regardless of the payor's income.

explanation: Under the Divorce Act s.2(1) defines 'child of the marriage' to include a person who is 18 or older and 'is unable, by reason of illness, disability, or other cause, to withdraw from their charge or to obtain the necessaries of life.' Courts have consistently held that full-time post-secondary attendance is an 'other cause' within this definition, preventing a young adult from withdrawing from parental charge. Child support for post-secondary students is calculated differently from table amounts — the court looks at the child's actual expenses, the parent's ability to pay, and any contribution the child can make (FCSG s.3(2)(b)). There is no automatic cutoff at age 21 and no fixed cap.
why_A_wrong: The Divorce Act does not impose a hard age-18 cutoff for child support. Section 2(1) expressly extends the definition of 'child of the marriage' to adults who cannot withdraw from parental charge, which includes post-secondary students.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. DA s.2(1) extends child support to adults in post-secondary education as a recognized 'other cause'; FCSG s.3(2)(b) governs quantum for such children.
why_C_wrong: There is no age-21 statutory cutoff for adult child support in Canada. Support may continue beyond age 21 where the child remains legitimately unable to withdraw from parental charge.
why_D_wrong: No statutory cap of $1,000/month applies to adult post-secondary child support. Quantum is assessed on the particular facts including the child's expenses, both parents' income, and any contribution the child makes.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Adult Children — Post-Secondary Education and Child Support
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 2(1); Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 3(2)(b)

tested_concepts: DA s.2(1) definition child of marriage

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Variation of Child Support — Material Change and Contino
fact_pattern: A child support order was made two years ago based on the payor's income of $75,000. The payor's income has now increased to $100,000. The recipient applies to vary the order to increase the table amount. The payor argues that the income increase alone is insufficient to constitute a 'material change in circumstances.'
call_of_question: What is the standard for varying a child support order under the Divorce Act, and does an increase in the payor's income satisfy it?

options.A: The payor is correct; a change in income alone is never a material change because the Guidelines produce a new table amount automatically without a court order.
options.B: Child support orders can be varied only if the child's needs have changed, not based solely on changes to the payor's income.
options.C: Under DA s.17(4) and s.17(6.1), a change in the condition, means, needs or other circumstances of either spouse or a child constitutes a material change warranting variation; an increase in the payor's income is itself a change in the payor's 'means' and is a recognized basis for upward variation of the table amount.
options.D: The increase from $75,000 to $100,000 is too modest to qualify as material; the income must increase by at least 25% before variation is available.

explanation: Under Divorce Act s.17(4), a court may vary a child support order where there has been a change in the condition, means, needs or other circumstances of the children or the spouse since the order was made. Under s.17(6.1), where the change is in income of the payor, the variation is made by recalculating the applicable table amount. A change in the payor's income is expressly recognized as a change in 'means' sufficient to constitute a material change, entitling the court to update the child support amount. There is no 25% minimum threshold.
why_A_wrong: Child support orders do not adjust automatically with income changes. A variation application to the court is required to obtain an order reflecting the new income. The Guidelines do not self-execute without a court order.
why_B_wrong: A change in the payor's income is expressly recognized by DA s.17(4) as a change in the payor's 'means' warranting variation. The child's needs need not have changed; the payor's improved means is independently sufficient.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. DA s.17(4) and s.17(6.1) recognize a change in the payor's income as a material change warranting variation; the table amount is then recalculated.
why_D_wrong: No 25% threshold exists in the Divorce Act or the Federal Child Support Guidelines. Any genuine, non-trivial change in income constitutes a change in 'means' for the purposes of s.17(4).

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Variation of Child Support — DA s.17
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17(4), s. 17(6.1); Contino v. Leonelli-Contino, [2005] 3 SCR 217

tested_concepts: payor income change as material change

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Shared Parenting — 40% Threshold for FCSG s.9
subtopic_heading: 40% Threshold — What Counts as Parenting Time for FCSG s.9
fact_pattern: A parenting order grants the mother 210 overnights per year and the father 155 overnights per year with their one child. The father argues that because he has the child for approximately 42% of overnights, FCSG s.9 applies and he should pay a reduced amount of child support rather than the full table amount. The mother argues the full table amount applies because s.9 should be calculated differently.
call_of_question: For the purpose of FCSG s.9, how is the 40% threshold calculated, and does the father meet it?

options.A: The 40% threshold is calculated based only on daytime hours, not overnights; 155 overnights is insufficient without counting daytime hours.
options.B: The 40% threshold under FCSG s.9 is measured over the course of a year; a parent who exercises parenting time for at least 40% of the time (approximately 146 days or more out of 365) meets the threshold; 155 overnights represents approximately 42% and satisfies the threshold.
options.C: The 40% threshold requires exactly 50/50 parenting time; anything less requires payment of the full table amount.
options.D: FCSG s.9 applies only if the child sleeps at each parent's home an equal number of nights; at 155 vs 210 overnights, s.9 is inapplicable.

explanation: Under the Federal Child Support Guidelines s.9 applies where 'a spouse exercises parenting time with a child for not less than 40 percent of the time over the course of a year.' The threshold is 40% — not 50%. Courts typically calculate the percentage based on the total number of days (or nights) in a year attributable to each parent. At 155 overnights out of 365 (approximately 42.5%), the father meets the s.9 threshold, and the court must apply the three-factor analysis from Contino v. Leonelli-Contino, [2005] 3 SCR 217 rather than simply imposing the full table amount. The calculation is not limited to overnights if there is significant daytime-only time, but overnights are the most common metric used.
why_A_wrong: Courts most commonly measure parenting time for FCSG s.9 purposes using overnights as a practical proxy for total time spent with each parent. Daytime hours can be included where relevant, but overnights are the standard measure; 155 overnights clearly exceeds 40% of the year.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.9 threshold is 40% or more of the year; 155 out of 365 days equals approximately 42.5%, satisfying the threshold and engaging the three-factor Contino analysis.
why_C_wrong: FCSG s.9 does not require equal (50/50) parenting time. The threshold is 40%, not 50%. Any parenting time at or above 40% engages s.9.
why_D_wrong: FCSG s.9 does not require equal overnight distribution. The 40% threshold can be met at any split above 40%, including 42% vs 58% as in this scenario.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Shared Parenting 40% Threshold — FCSG s.9
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 9; Contino v. Leonelli-Contino, [2005] 3 SCR 217

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: FCSG s.3(2)(b) — Quantum for Adult Children Over Age of Majority
fact_pattern: Noah turned 18 last September and began a full-time four-year engineering degree program in September. He lives on campus during the school year and returns to his mother's home during breaks. He has no employment income. His parents divorced when he was 12; the existing order requires his father to pay table-amount child support of $1,200/month based on the father's income at the time. The father brings a motion to terminate the child support order on the basis that Noah is now an adult. The mother cross-moves to vary the order to address Noah's current university costs of $21,500/year.
call_of_question: How should the court approach child support for Noah as a full-time university student over the age of majority?

options.A: Child support terminates automatically at age 18; the father's motion to terminate succeeds and no variation is required.
options.B: The existing $1,200/month table-amount order continues unchanged; the table amount is the mandatory amount for all children regardless of age.
options.C: Under FCSG s.3(2)(b) and DA s.2(1), Noah remains a 'child of the marriage' unable to withdraw from parental charge due to full-time studies; the table amount is not automatically applied; the court must assess the appropriateness of the table amount or an alternative amount based on Noah's condition, means, needs, and circumstances and each parent's financial ability to contribute — typically the actual post-secondary costs apportioned proportionately between parents.
options.D: Support for adult children is capped at a maximum of $1,000/month regardless of actual expenses or the table amount.

explanation: Under Divorce Act s.2(1), a 'child of the marriage' includes an adult child unable to withdraw from parental charge by reason of, among other things, pursuit of reasonably required education — which courts consistently apply to full-time post-secondary studies. Termination at age 18 is therefore incorrect. Under FCSG s.3(2)(b), once a child reaches the age of majority, the mandatory table-amount rule under s.3(1) no longer applies automatically; the court must consider whether the table amount is appropriate having regard to the child's condition, means, needs, and circumstances and each parent's ability to contribute. For a university student living away from home, courts regularly use the student's actual post-secondary costs as the starting point, apportion them between the parents proportionate to their incomes, and may also maintain a contribution to the residential parent's household costs. The table amount is one option — not the only one.
why_A_wrong: General Rule Trap. Child support does not terminate automatically at age 18. Under DA s.2(1), a child pursuing full-time post-secondary education remains a 'child of the marriage' unable to withdraw from parental charge, and support continues subject to the FCSG s.3(2)(b) framework.
why_B_wrong: General Rule Trap (continued application). FCSG s.3(1)'s mandatory table-amount rule governs children under the age of majority. Once the child reaches majority, s.3(2)(b) replaces it with an appropriateness assessment. The table amount is not mandatory for adult children — it is one possible outcome of the analysis.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Noah remains a child of the marriage under DA s.2(1); FCSG s.3(2)(b) requires an appropriateness assessment rather than automatic table-amount application; actual post-secondary costs apportioned proportionately is the typical approach.
why_D_wrong: Overstatement Trap (inverted — Understatement). There is no $1,000/month cap on adult child support. FCSG s.3(2)(b) contains no monetary ceiling; the amount is determined by the appropriateness analysis and may exceed the table amount for a university student with significant costs.

source_reference.heading: Adult Child Support — FCSG s.3(2)(b)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 3(2)(b); Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 2(1)

FCSG s.3(2)(b) appropriateness assessment replaces mandatory table
no dollar cap on adult child support

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Retroactive Child Support — DBS v SRG Factors
fact_pattern: Aaron and Bianca separated six years ago. A child support order was made at that time based on Aaron's income of $60,000. Aaron's income has since increased to $100,000, but he never voluntarily disclosed this to Bianca. Bianca learned of Aaron's income increase only recently and now brings a variation application seeking retroactive child support for the past four years.
call_of_question: Under the framework in DBS v SRG, [2006] 2 SCR 231, what factors govern whether and how far back a retroactive child support award should be made?

options.A: Retroactive child support is not available; child support can only be increased from the date of the variation application.
options.B: Under DBS v SRG, a court may award retroactive child support, taking into account: the reason for the recipient's delay in seeking support; the conduct of the payor (including non-disclosure of income); the circumstances of the child; and hardship to the payor from retroactive payment.
options.C: Retroactive child support is automatically awarded for the full limitation period of six years before the application.
options.D: Retroactive child support may only be awarded for one year prior to the application, with no exceptions.

explanation: In DBS v SRG, [2006] 2 SCR 231, the Supreme Court of Canada established the analytical framework for retroactive child support. The four factors are: (1) whether the recipient had a reasonable excuse for the delay in bringing the application; (2) whether the payor engaged in blameworthy conduct (such as non-disclosure of income increases); (3) the circumstances of the child and whether they will benefit from a retroactive award; and (4) whether the payor would suffer hardship from the retroactive award. The Court also established a general 'rule of thumb' that retroactive awards should not generally extend more than three years before the date of the formal claim, but this may be exceeded where there is payor misconduct (such as non-disclosure). Aaron's consistent non-disclosure of income is blameworthy conduct supporting a more generous retroactive award.
why_A_wrong: Retroactive child support is available in Canadian family law and was confirmed by the Supreme Court in DBS v SRG. Courts regularly award retroactive support where justified by the four-factor analysis.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. DBS v SRG establishes four factors: delay reason, payor blameworthy conduct, child's circumstances, and hardship to payor; non-disclosure supports a retroactive award.
why_C_wrong: There is no automatic full-limitation-period retroactivity. Courts apply the four-factor DBS analysis and generally apply a three-year presumptive period, which may be extended for payor misconduct.
why_D_wrong: A one-year limit on retroactive child support is not the rule established in DBS v SRG. The general starting point is three years before the formal claim, subject to extension for blameworthy payor conduct.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Retroactive Child Support — DBS v SRG
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: DBS v SRG; LJW v TAR; Henry v Henry; Hiemstra v Hiemstra, [2006] 2 SCR 231

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Tax Treatment — Child Support Payments Not Deductible/Not Income
fact_pattern: In 1995, a court order required Kwame to pay $800/month in child support to his former spouse Amara. Under that order, Kwame deducted the payments from his income and Amara included them as taxable income — consistent with the rules at the time. In 2022, the parties returned to court and the order was varied to increase child support to $1,400/month due to Kwame's increased income. Kwame's accountant now advises him that the 1995 order predates the 1997 amendments so he may continue to deduct all child support payments under the old regime. Amara's accountant advises her to continue including all payments as income.
call_of_question: Which tax regime applies to Kwame's child support payments following the 2022 variation order?

options.A: The pre-1997 regime (deductible by payor, taxable to recipient) continues to govern all payments because the original order was made in 1995, before the May 1997 amendments; the variation does not affect the tax treatment.
options.B: Following the 2022 variation order, a 'commencement day' is triggered under the Income Tax Act; from that date, all child support payments — including the base amount — are no longer deductible by Kwame and are no longer taxable income to Amara.
options.C: Only the incremental $600/month increase is tax-neutral under the post-1997 regime; the original $800/month base amount remains deductible by Kwame and taxable to Amara under the grandfathered 1995 order.
options.D: The 2022 variation is a new order and creates a new tax regime only for future orders; Kwame may elect which regime applies by filing an election with CRA within 60 days of the variation.

explanation: Under the Income Tax Act establishes the concept of a 'commencement day' to determine which tax regime applies to child support payments. Under ITA s.56.1(4) and s.60.1(4), a commencement day is triggered when a pre-May 1997 order or agreement is varied after April 1997 — specifically, the commencement day is the effective date of the variation. Once a commencement day arises, all child support amounts payable under the agreement or order on and after that day fall under the post-1997 tax-neutral regime: the payments are not deductible by the payor (ITA s.60(b)) and not includible as income by the recipient (ITA s.56(1)(b)). The variation does not split the treatment between old and new amounts — the commencement day converts the entire ongoing child support obligation to the tax-neutral regime. Both accountants' advice is incorrect after the 2022 variation.
why_A_wrong: Wrong Timeline Trap. While the 1995 order itself was governed by the pre-1997 regime, the ITA commencement day rules specifically address variations. A variation of a pre-1997 order made after April 1997 triggers a commencement day, ending the old deductible/taxable regime for all payments going forward.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The 2022 variation triggers a commencement day under ITA s.56.1(4)/s.60.1(4), converting all child support payments — both the original base and the increased amount — to the post-1997 tax-neutral regime from that date.
why_C_wrong: Misapplied Tool. The commencement day rules do not split child support into two streams (old regime base plus new regime increment). Once a commencement day is triggered by a variation, the post-1997 tax-neutral regime applies to all child support payable under the order from the commencement day forward — not just the incremental amount.
why_D_wrong: Exception Trap. No CRA election mechanism allows payors to choose which tax regime applies following a variation that triggers a commencement day. The commencement day rules operate automatically under the ITA; there is no opt-in or opt-out election available.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Tax Treatment of Child Support Payments
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Income Tax Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 1 (5th Supp.), ss. 56(1)(b), 56.1(4), 60(b), 60.1(4) (Commencement Day rules)

tested_concepts: ITA commencement day triggered by post-1997 variation
all payments become tax-neutral after commencement day
no split regime between base and increment

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Undue Hardship Claim — Criteria Under FCSG s.10
fact_pattern: Derek earns $75,000/year and has two children from his current marriage who live with him. The Federal Child Support Guidelines table amount for his two children from his former marriage would be $1,200/month. He argues that paying $1,200/month in addition to supporting his new family creates undue hardship and he should pay less. His former spouse disputes this.
call_of_question: Under FCSG s.10, what must Derek establish to succeed on an undue hardship claim, and what additional requirement must be satisfied?

options.A: Derek must prove that paying the table amount would leave him with less than $500/month for personal expenses.
options.B: Under FCSG s.10, the court may award a different amount where the table amount would cause undue hardship to either spouse; Derek must: (1) establish one of the specified circumstances in s.10(2) (including unusually high debts or legal duties to support another person); and (2) show that his household standard of living is lower than the recipient's household standard of living.
options.C: Derek's new family obligations automatically establish undue hardship, requiring a reduction of child support.
options.D: Undue hardship is established solely by showing that the table amount exceeds 20% of the payor's income.

explanation: Under the Federal Child Support Guidelines s.10 provides that a court may award a different amount where the table amount would cause undue hardship to the paying or receiving spouse. Two cumulative elements must be established: (1) one of the circumstances listed in s.10(2), which includes unusually high debts from the marriage, unusually high expenses for exercising parenting time, and legal duties to support another person (such as children from a subsequent relationship); and (2) the household standard of living test — the court will not grant an undue hardship claim if the payor's household standard of living is higher than the recipient's. Derek's support obligations to his new family may qualify under s.10(2)(d) (legal duty to support other children), but he must also show his household standard of living is not higher than his former spouse's.
why_A_wrong: No $500/month threshold test exists in FCSG s.10. The undue hardship analysis requires establishing a listed circumstance in s.10(2) and passing the household standard of living comparison test.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.10 requires two cumulative elements: a s.10(2) listed circumstance and a lower household standard of living than the recipient's; both must be met.
why_C_wrong: New family obligations do not automatically establish undue hardship. They are one of the listed circumstances (s.10(2)(d)) but are only sufficient if the household standard of living test is also satisfied.
why_D_wrong: No 20% income threshold test is found in FCSG s.10. The undue hardship test is qualitative and requires analysis of specified circumstances and the comparative household standard of living test.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Undue Hardship — FCSG s.10
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 10(1), s. 10(2)

tested_concepts: FCSG s.10 undue hardship two-part test

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Step-Parent Obligation to Pay Child Support — FCSG s.5
fact_pattern: Helen and George were married for 6 years and have now separated. During the marriage, George treated Helen's 8-year-old daughter from a previous relationship as his own child — paying for her school activities, taking her to appointments, and acting as a parental figure. George has no biological children. Helen now seeks child support from George for her daughter under the Divorce Act. George argues he has no legal obligation to pay support because he is not the child's biological father.
call_of_question: Under what legal basis might George be obligated to pay child support for Helen's daughter in a Divorce Act proceeding, and what standard governs?

options.A: George has no obligation; only biological parents or legal adoptive parents are required to pay child support.
options.B: Under FCSG s.5 and Divorce Act s.2(2), a step-parent who stands in the place of a parent may have an obligation to pay child support; the amount is determined by the court considering all relevant circumstances, as the table amount may not reflect the appropriate level of step-parent support given that the biological parent may also have an obligation.
options.C: A step-parent is automatically equally liable for child support as the biological parent, and George must pay the full table amount.
options.D: George's child support obligation arises only if he formally adopted the child.

explanation: Under Divorce Act s.2(2), a person who stands in the place of a parent (in loco parentis) has obligations similar to those of a parent during the period they occupy that role. Federal Child Support Guidelines s.5 specifically addresses step-parent child support: where the 'spouse' (in the DA context, a person who stands in place of a parent) is obligated to pay support, the court may award an amount that the court considers appropriate having regard to the Guidelines and any other parent's legal duty to support the child. The court balances the step-parent's resources against the biological parent's obligation, often resulting in a lesser amount than the full table amount. Six years of acting as a de facto parent supports a finding that George stands in the place of a parent.
why_A_wrong: The Divorce Act and FCSG expressly recognize obligations of persons who stand in the place of a parent (step-parents in the de facto parental role). Legal obligation is not limited to biological or adoptive parents.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.5 and DA s.2(2) recognize step-parent child support obligations where the step-parent has stood in the place of a parent; the amount is determined by the court considering all circumstances.
why_C_wrong: Step-parents are not automatically equally liable as biological parents at the full table amount. FCSG s.5 directs the court to determine an 'appropriate' amount having regard to the Guidelines and the biological parent's obligations — typically resulting in a lesser amount.
why_D_wrong: Formal adoption is not required to establish a step-parent obligation. A person who has functioned as a parent in a de facto sense for a significant period stands in the place of a parent under DA s.2(2) without the need for formal adoption.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Step-Parent Child Support — FCSG s.5 and DA s.2(2)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 5; Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 2(2)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Income Above $150,000 — FCSG s.4 Departure from Table Amount
fact_pattern: Dr. Sharma earns $500,000 per year as a surgeon. He and his former spouse have two children aged 8 and 11 who live primarily with the mother. Under the Ontario Schedule I table, the table amount for a $150,000 income with two children is $1,980/month. Dr. Sharma argues that the court must award the table amount at the $150,000 benchmark since the table does not extend beyond $150,000. The mother argues the court should award more.
call_of_question: How does FCSG s.4 address child support where the payor's annual income exceeds $150,000?

options.A: The table amount at $150,000 is the maximum; a payor earning more than $150,000 pays no more than the table amount at that income level.
options.B: Under FCSG s.4, where the payor's income exceeds $150,000, the court has two options: (a) award the table amount for $150,000 plus an additional amount the court considers appropriate; or (b) if the table amount for $150,000 does not appropriately reflect the children's needs and the payor's ability to pay, award any amount it considers appropriate.
options.C: For income above $150,000, child support is automatically capped at 2% of income regardless of the table amount.
options.D: The court must apply the full proportional table formula by extrapolating beyond $150,000 as if the table extended indefinitely.

explanation: Under the Federal Child Support Guidelines s.4 provides that where the payor's income exceeds $150,000, the court shall award: (a) the amount shown in the table for the $150,000 income level, plus an additional amount the court considers appropriate; or (b) if the table amount for $150,000 does not appropriately reflect the condition, means, needs, and circumstances of the children and the payor's ability to pay, any amount that the court considers appropriate. The SSAG for high-income payors recognizes that the table amounts for lower incomes may not adequately reflect what is appropriate when the payor has very high means. In Dr. Sharma's case, a court would likely award substantially more than the $1,980 table amount for $150,000.
why_A_wrong: FCSG s.4 does not cap child support at the $150,000 table amount for high-income payors. The court has discretion under s.4 to award additional amounts above the $150,000 benchmark.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.4 provides two options for income above $150,000: table amount plus additional appropriate amount, or a fully discretionary amount if the table amount is not appropriate.
why_C_wrong: There is no 2% income cap formula in the Federal Child Support Guidelines. The FCSG does not contain such a provision for high-income payors.
why_D_wrong: The table does not extend beyond $150,000; the court does not extrapolate. Instead, FCSG s.4 provides the two-option framework for incomes above $150,000.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: High-Income Payor — FCSG s.4
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 4

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: FCSG s.19 — Grounds for Imputing Income and Burden of Proof
fact_pattern: Priya earns $95,000 per year as a software engineer. She decides to leave her position and pursue painting full-time, earning approximately $8,000 per year. She and her former partner share two children, and he brings a child support variation application. Priya argues her income for Guidelines purposes is $8,000. Her former partner argues the court should impute income to her.
call_of_question: Under FCSG s.19(1), on what grounds may the court impute income to Priya, and what level of income may it impute?

options.A: The court cannot impute income to a parent who has changed careers; career choices are a matter of personal autonomy and the court must accept actual income.
options.B: Under FCSG s.19(1)(a), the court may impute income where a spouse is intentionally under-employed or unemployed; if Priya's career change results in significantly lower income and is not appropriate given her education, experience, and the ability to earn more, the court may impute income at a level reflecting her notional earning capacity — typically the income she was earning or could reasonably earn.
options.C: The court must impute the maximum possible income to Priya regardless of her career decision.
options.D: Income imputation is only available for unemployed persons, not for those who are self-employed in a new career.

explanation: Under the Federal Child Support Guidelines s.19(1)(a) permits the court to impute income to a paying spouse who is intentionally under-employed or unemployed, unless the situation is 'reasonable' given the circumstances (such as illness, disability, or a reasonable career change that is not designed to avoid child support). Courts assess whether the career change is genuine and reasonable, considering the parent's education, skills, prior income, and the impact on the children. Priya's dramatic income reduction from $95,000 to $8,000 — a career choice she had the freedom to make personally — is subject to scrutiny if it appears connected to avoiding child support obligations. The court would typically impute income at or near Priya's previous earning level unless she can justify the change as reasonable and not motivated by avoiding support.
why_A_wrong: Personal autonomy in career choices does not shield a parent from income imputation where the choice results in intentional underemployment at the expense of child support obligations. FCSG s.19(1)(a) applies to voluntary career changes that amount to intentional underemployment.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.19(1)(a) allows imputation for intentional underemployment; the court may impute income at Priya's earning capacity level (approximately $95,000) unless she can establish the career change is appropriate in the circumstances.
why_C_wrong: The court does not impute the maximum possible income regardless of circumstances. It imputes income at a level reflecting the parent's notional earning capacity — what they could reasonably earn given their qualifications — not an unrealistic maximum.
why_D_wrong: Section 19(1)(a) applies to both unemployed and under-employed persons. A self-employed person who is earning far below their capacity — as Priya appears to be — may be found to be intentionally under-employed and subject to income imputation.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Imputing Income — FCSG s.19(1)(a)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 19(1)(a)

tested_concepts: burden on payor to justify reasonableness

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Imputation Where Payor Relocates to Lower-Wage Region — FCSG s.19(1)(a) Discretion
fact_pattern: Kevin was an accountant earning $110,000 per year in Toronto. Following separation, he moves to a rural community in Northern Ontario and accepts an accounting position paying $52,000 per year, claiming he prefers rural life and lower cost of living. His former spouse applies to impute income to him at $110,000. Kevin argues the move was genuine and personal.
call_of_question: Is Kevin's voluntary relocation to a lower-wage region with resulting income reduction subject to income imputation under FCSG s.19?

options.A: Kevin's relocation is a personal lifestyle choice that the court cannot second-guess; his income for child support purposes is $52,000.
options.B: Under FCSG s.19(1)(a), a court may impute income where a spouse is intentionally under-employed; a voluntary move to a lower-wage area that results in a significant income reduction is subject to scrutiny; the court weighs whether the move is appropriate in the circumstances — personal preference for rural lifestyle is a factor but not automatically determinative; the court may impute income at a level between the current wage and prior earning capacity.
options.C: Any relocation that reduces income automatically results in imputation at the prior income level.
options.D: Imputation is never available for relocation-based income reduction; only resignation from employment triggers s.19.

explanation: Under the FCSG s.19(1)(a) does not prohibit a parent from relocating or changing positions, but it permits the court to impute income where the result is intentional underemployment. Courts apply a contextual assessment: whether the decision is reasonable in all the circumstances, including the impact on child support obligations. A voluntary move from a $110,000 position to $52,000 — nearly halving one's income — will attract scrutiny. The court considers whether the move is genuine (preference for rural life, family connection to the area, genuine lifestyle change) versus strategic (timed to post-separation, designed to reduce support). The court has discretion to impute at a level it considers appropriate, which may be the original income, the mid-point, or another figure reflecting what Kevin could reasonably earn.
why_A_wrong: Personal lifestyle choices do not automatically insulate a parent from income imputation. The court assesses whether the lifestyle choice constitutes intentional underemployment in the circumstances, balancing the parent's autonomy against the children's right to support.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.19(1)(a) applies to voluntary relocation that results in significant income reduction; the court weighs the reasonableness of the move and may impute at a level reflecting earning capacity; the outcome is not automatic in either direction.
why_C_wrong: Not all relocations resulting in income reduction lead to imputation at the prior income level. The court has discretion and applies a reasonableness assessment. A truly justified relocation with a genuine personal rationale may not attract full imputation.
why_D_wrong: FCSG s.19(1)(a) is not limited to resignation from employment. Any situation of intentional underemployment — including a voluntary move to a lower-paying market — may attract imputation if found to be unreasonable in the circumstances.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Imputing Income — Relocation and Underemployment
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 19(1)(a)

tested_concepts: relocation income reduction subject to s.19 scrutiny

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Income Imputation — Unreported Income and Pattern of Overspending
fact_pattern: Fernando declares $42,000 in annual income for tax purposes. However, his lifestyle — a luxury car lease, vacations, restaurant spending, and expensive hobbies — is clearly inconsistent with $42,000. Bank records show deposits of approximately $90,000. His former spouse brings a child support application and seeks imputation of income. Fernando claims all excess deposits are loans from family.
call_of_question: Under FCSG s.19, what grounds beyond intentional underemployment are available for imputing income to Fernando?

options.A: The court must accept Fernando's declared income and tax returns; they cannot look behind official documents.
options.B: Under FCSG s.19(1)(b) and (c), income may be imputed where a spouse has failed to provide income information or where the court determines the income available to the spouse is different from declared income; lifestyle inconsistency (spending far exceeding declared income) and bank deposits significantly exceeding declared income are recognized bases for imputing income at a level consistent with the payor's actual financial capacity.
options.C: Imputation based on lifestyle or bank deposits requires a criminal conviction for tax evasion.
options.D: The court must accept the declared income unless the recipient can independently prove the exact amount of unreported income.

explanation: Under the Federal Child Support Guidelines s.19(1) lists multiple grounds for income imputation beyond intentional underemployment. Section 19(1)(h) permits imputation where a spouse earns income from a business that is not reflected in the declared income. Courts also routinely use lifestyle evidence — expenditures inconsistent with declared income — as grounds to impute income at a level consistent with the lifestyle demonstrated. Bank deposits substantially exceeding declared income are powerful evidence supporting imputation. Fernando bears no automatic benefit of doubt; the court may impute income at a level reflecting his apparent actual financial capacity based on the totality of evidence. No criminal conviction is required — this is a civil proceeding applying a balance of probabilities standard.
why_A_wrong: Courts are not bound to accept tax returns or declared income at face value when lifestyle evidence and bank records are inconsistent with declared income. FCSG s.19 specifically authorizes the court to look behind declared income.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.19 authorizes imputation based on lifestyle inconsistency and bank deposit evidence; the court may impute income at a level reflecting Fernando's actual financial capacity; civil standard of proof applies.
why_C_wrong: No criminal conviction is required. Income imputation in child support proceedings is governed by the civil standard of balance of probabilities. Evidence of lifestyle inconsistency is evaluated as civil evidence, not criminal.
why_D_wrong: The recipient is not required to independently prove the exact amount of unreported income. The court assesses the evidence holistically and may draw inferences from lifestyle and bank deposit evidence; the burden of explanation then falls on Fernando to provide a credible account of the discrepancy.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Income Imputation — Unreported Income and Lifestyle Evidence
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 19(1)

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Special and Extraordinary Expenses — FCSG s.7
subtopic_heading: s.7 Extracurricular Activities — Threshold Test for 'Extraordinary'
fact_pattern: Ten-year-old Lily attends weekly piano lessons at $60 per lesson and plays recreational soccer with registration fees of $300 per year. Her mother seeks an order requiring the father to contribute to both as s.7 special/extraordinary expenses. The father argues that neither qualifies because they are ordinary extracurricular activities.
call_of_question: Under FCSG s.7, what is the threshold for extracurricular activities to qualify as 'extraordinary' expenses requiring proportionate contribution from both parents?

options.A: All extracurricular activities automatically qualify as s.7 expenses regardless of cost.
options.B: Under FCSG s.7(1)(f), extracurricular activities qualify as s.7 expenses if they are 'extraordinary' given the family's circumstances; the test considers whether the expense is excessive compared to the family's budget and lifestyle, and whether it is beyond what is 'ordinary' for a family with their income; routine, low-cost activities like recreational soccer typically do not qualify, while elite competitive programs or unusually expensive activities may.
options.C: Extracurricular activities are never s.7 expenses; they are the exclusive responsibility of the parent in whose primary care the child lives.
options.D: Any extracurricular activity costing more than $100 per month qualifies as an extraordinary expense under s.7.

explanation: Under the Federal Child Support Guidelines s.7(1)(f) includes 'extraordinary extracurricular activities' as a potential s.7 expense. 'Extraordinary' is assessed contextually: the activity must be unusual in nature or cost given the family's income, lifestyle, and circumstances. Low-cost recreational activities (like a $300/year soccer registration or modest piano lessons) that are typical of families with the parties' income level generally do not qualify as 'extraordinary.' Elite competitive programs, travel teams with significant costs, or activities well beyond what the family would normally afford may qualify. The Guidelines and case law provide no bright-line dollar threshold; the assessment is proportional and contextual. On these facts, recreational soccer likely does not qualify; piano lessons at $60/lesson may be borderline depending on the frequency and the parties' incomes.
why_A_wrong: Section 7(1)(f) requires extracurricular activities to be 'extraordinary' — not merely extracurricular. Ordinary, low-cost recreational activities are not automatically s.7 expenses.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The 'extraordinary' threshold under s.7(1)(f) requires the activity to be unusual or excessive given the family's income and circumstances; ordinary recreational activities like recreational soccer typically do not qualify.
why_C_wrong: While the table amount is intended to cover 'ordinary' expenses, s.7(1)(f) expressly provides for extraordinary extracurricular activities to be shared. The primary caregiver does not bear these costs exclusively; they are shared proportionally if the 'extraordinary' threshold is met.
why_D_wrong: There is no $100/month or any other fixed dollar threshold in FCSG s.7 for determining whether an extracurricular activity is 'extraordinary.' The assessment is contextual and income-sensitive, not based on a fixed dollar amount.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Section 7 — Extraordinary Extracurricular Activities
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 7(1)(f)

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Special and Extraordinary Expenses — FCSG s.7
subtopic_heading: s.7 Post-Secondary Education Expenses — Adult Child's Expected Contribution
fact_pattern: Jordan is 20 years old and in his second year of university. His parents are divorced. His mother applies for an order requiring his father to pay all of Jordan's tuition ($9,000/year), books ($1,200/year), and residence ($13,000/year). Jordan works part-time and earns $7,000/year. The father argues Jordan should contribute his own earnings to his education costs before the parents are obligated to share the balance.
call_of_question: Under the Federal Child Support Guidelines framework for adult child support, is Jordan expected to contribute his own earnings to his post-secondary expenses?

options.A: No — adult children in post-secondary education are not expected to contribute any of their own resources; both parents share 100% of all costs proportionally.
options.B: Yes — under the framework applicable to adult children under FCSG s.3(2)(b) and case law, the court considers the child's own resources and earning capacity as a factor in determining the appropriate level of parental contribution; Jordan's part-time earnings and reasonable contribution from summer work are typically deducted before the parents' proportionate shares of the remaining expenses are calculated.
options.C: Jordan may only be required to contribute if he has a scholarship or bursary; part-time wages are irrelevant.
options.D: Adult children are always expected to fund 50% of their post-secondary expenses regardless of parental income.

explanation: Under the Federal Child Support Guidelines s.3(2)(b) provides that for adult children, the court shall consider the condition, means, needs, and other circumstances of both the child and the spouses in determining an appropriate support amount. Case law consistently holds that adult children in post-secondary education are expected to contribute their own resources — including part-time earnings, summer employment income, and government student loans — before the parents are required to divide the remaining costs proportionally. Jordan's $7,000 in part-time earnings would typically be attributed to his education costs first, reducing the amount the parents must share. This reflects the policy that adult children bear primary responsibility for their own education, with parental support as a supplement.
why_A_wrong: Adult children are not insulated from contributing their own resources to post-secondary expenses. FCSG s.3(2)(b) requires the court to consider the child's own means, and case law applies a principle that the adult child contributes before parents divide the balance.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.3(2)(b) requires consideration of the adult child's own resources; Jordan's part-time earnings reduce the amount the parents share; this reflects the policy that adult children contribute before parental support is calculated.
why_C_wrong: The child's own contribution is not limited to scholarships and bursaries. Part-time wages, summer employment, and student loans are all considered. Jordan's $7,000 in part-time income is directly relevant.
why_D_wrong: There is no fixed 50% rule for adult child contributions. The contribution is assessed based on the child's actual resources and earning capacity, not a fixed percentage. Parents with different incomes share the remaining balance proportionally after the child's contribution.

source_reference.heading: Adult Child Post-Secondary — Child's Contribution Expected
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 3(2)(b)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Undue Hardship — Household Standard of Living Comparison Test
fact_pattern: Troy earns $60,000 per year. He pays child support for two children from a previous relationship ($1,100/month). He has two additional children from his new marriage who live with him, and his new spouse earns $30,000. He claims undue hardship from paying $1,100/month. The recipient parent (his former partner) is a single parent earning $45,000. Troy argues his new family obligations satisfy the undue hardship test.
call_of_question: Under FCSG s.10, does Troy satisfy the two-part undue hardship test on these facts?

options.A: Troy satisfies the test because he has legal duties to support children in his new family, which is one of the s.10(2) circumstances.
options.B: Troy may satisfy the first part of the FCSG s.10 test (demonstrating a listed s.10(2) circumstance — legal duty to support other dependants), but he also must satisfy the second part: that his household standard of living is lower than the recipient's; with household income of $90,000 ($60,000 + $30,000) for his current family vs. the former spouse's $45,000, Troy's household is likely better off and the s.10 test is not fully met.
options.C: Troy automatically satisfies the undue hardship test because he supports more than two children in total.
options.D: Undue hardship is only available if the payor is unemployed; Troy's continued employment at $60,000 disqualifies him from claiming hardship.

explanation: Under the Federal Child Support Guidelines s.10 requires two cumulative elements for undue hardship: (1) the existence of one of the s.10(2) listed circumstances — including legal duties to support other dependants (s.10(2)(d)); and (2) that the household standard of living of the claimant's household (Troy's) is lower than the recipient's household. Troy satisfies the first element: he has legal duties to support his two new children. However, the second element — the household standard of living comparison — is critical. Troy's household earns $90,000 combined vs. the former spouse's $45,000 as a single parent. Courts assess household income per capita or using an equivalence scale, but on these rough numbers Troy's household appears better off financially than the recipient's, likely defeating the s.10 claim.
why_A_wrong: Satisfying one of the s.10(2) circumstances is necessary but not sufficient for undue hardship. The household standard of living comparison (second element) must also be met. Troy fails the second element on these facts.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Troy satisfies s.10(2)(d) but fails the household standard of living test because his combined household income ($90,000) exceeds the former spouse's household income ($45,000); the claim is unlikely to succeed.
why_C_wrong: No automatic undue hardship rule exists based on total number of children. Both elements of the FCSG s.10 test must be met; the number of children supported is relevant only to establish the s.10(2) circumstance, not to automatically establish the full test.
why_D_wrong: Undue hardship is not limited to unemployed payors. FCSG s.10 applies to any payor who meets both elements of the test regardless of employment status. Continued employment does not disqualify a hardship claim.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Undue Hardship — Household Standard of Living Test
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 10(1), s. 10(2)(d)
