subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Undue Hardship — Unusually High Debt Obligations as Listed Circumstance
fact_pattern: Warren separated from his spouse three years ago. During the marriage, he incurred $80,000 in joint debts to cover the family's living expenses (mostly credit card and line of credit) that he is solely responsible for under their separation agreement. He pays $1,800/month on these debts. He earns $72,000. He argues the debt obligation creates undue hardship in paying the full table amount of child support of $1,300/month.
call_of_question: Does Warren's debt obligation to service marriage-related debts qualify as a listed circumstance under FCSG s.10(2)?

options.A: No — debt obligations are not listed in s.10(2) and cannot ground an undue hardship claim.
options.B: Yes — under FCSG s.10(2)(b), unusually high debts reasonably incurred to support the family prior to separation or to earn a living are a listed s.10(2) circumstance that may ground an undue hardship claim; Warren must still satisfy the household standard of living comparison test.
options.C: Any debt, however incurred, qualifies as a listed circumstance under s.10(2).
options.D: Marriage-related debts are always the responsibility of the recipient of child support, not the payor.

explanation: Under the Federal Child Support Guidelines s.10(2)(b) lists 'unusually high debts reasonably incurred to support the family prior to separation or to earn a living' as a circumstance that may ground an undue hardship claim. Warren's $80,000 in debt incurred to support the family during the marriage, for which he is solely responsible, potentially qualifies under this provision. However, the analysis is two-step: (1) the s.10(2)(b) circumstance must be established; and (2) Warren's household standard of living must be lower than the recipient's. The debts must be 'unusually high' relative to his income — here, $80,000 in debt ($1,800/month payments on a $72,000 income) may qualify as unusually high. The second (household comparison) element still requires separate analysis.
why_A_wrong: FCSG s.10(2)(b) expressly includes unusually high marriage-related debts as a listed circumstance. Debts are a recognized ground for undue hardship claims, provided they meet the 'unusually high' threshold.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FCSG s.10(2)(b) lists unusually high marriage-related debts as a ground; Warren must also satisfy the household standard of living comparison test as the second cumulative element.
why_C_wrong: Not every debt qualifies — s.10(2)(b) requires the debt to be 'unusually high' and 'reasonably incurred' in connection with supporting the family or earning a living. Ordinary debts at typical levels do not automatically qualify.
why_D_wrong: There is no rule assigning marriage-related debts to the child support recipient. The debts' treatment depends on the separation agreement or court order. On these facts, Warren is solely responsible under their agreement, making the debt his obligation for undue hardship purposes.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Undue Hardship — Debt Obligations FCSG s.10(2)(b)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175, s. 10(2)(b)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: DBS Four-Factor Framework — Three-Year Presumption and Payor Misconduct
fact_pattern: Elena and Marco separated five years ago. A child support order was made at that time based on Marco's income of $80,000. Marco's income increased to $130,000 three years ago but he never disclosed this to Elena or the court. Elena discovered the income increase six months ago and now seeks retroactive child support for the past four years. Marco argues that retroactive support should not go back more than one year.
call_of_question: Under the DBS v SRG framework, to what extent may Elena's retroactive child support claim extend given Marco's non-disclosure?

options.A: Retroactive child support cannot go back more than one year in any case.
options.B: Under DBS v SRG, [2006] 2 SCR 231, the general presumptive period for retroactive child support is three years before the date of the formal claim; however, where the payor engaged in blameworthy conduct — specifically, failure to disclose income increases — the court may extend the retroactive period beyond three years; Marco's four-year non-disclosure is blameworthy conduct that may justify going back further than the three-year presumption.
options.C: Retroactive support automatically covers the full period of underpayment regardless of any time limit.
options.D: Non-disclosure of income increases is not blameworthy conduct for DBS purposes; Marco must have actively lied about income for the extended period to apply.

explanation: In DBS v SRG, [2006] 2 SCR 231, the Supreme Court of Canada held that retroactive child support may be awarded and established a general presumptive starting point: retroactive support should not normally go back more than three years before the date of the formal claim. However, where the payor engaged in 'blameworthy conduct' — which expressly includes failing to disclose income increases, as payors are under a positive obligation to do so — the court may award retroactive support beyond the three-year presumptive period. Marco's failure to disclose a $50,000 income increase for four years is blameworthy conduct, potentially justifying retroactive support going back the full four years to when the increase occurred. The retroactive award is not punitive; it is a recognition of what should have been paid.
why_A_wrong: There is no one-year absolute cap on retroactive child support under DBS v SRG. The general starting point is three years, and blameworthy conduct can extend the period further. Marco's argument of a one-year cap is incorrect.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. DBS v SRG establishes a three-year presumptive period with the option to go further for blameworthy conduct; non-disclosure of income is blameworthy conduct; Marco's four-year non-disclosure supports extending the retroactive period.
why_C_wrong: Retroactive support does not automatically cover the full underpayment period without limit. The DBS framework establishes a presumptive three-year period, adjusted for the four factors. Courts exercise discretion and may decline to go back the full period even where some blameworthy conduct exists.
why_D_wrong: Blameworthy conduct under DBS v SRG expressly includes failure to disclose income increases — payors have a positive obligation to disclose material changes in income. Active lying is not required; simple failure to disclose a known income increase constitutes blameworthy conduct.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Retroactive Child Support — DBS v SRG Four Factors
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: DBS v SRG; LJW v TAR; Henry v Henry; Hiemstra v Hiemstra, [2006] 2 SCR 231

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Retroactive Variation — Payor Lost Employment and Recipient Delayed Application
fact_pattern: Theo lost his job four years ago and his income dropped from $75,000 to $22,000. He did not bring a variation application at the time, hoping to find equivalent employment. He recently found new employment at $65,000. His former spouse April now applies to increase child support based on his new income. Theo counter-claims for a retroactive reduction for the four years of reduced income. April argues the retroactive reduction should not be granted.
call_of_question: Under the DBS v SRG framework, is a retroactive reduction of child support available where a payor had lower income but delayed bringing a variation application?

options.A: Retroactive reductions are always available for any period of lower income; Theo automatically receives a four-year retroactive reduction.
options.B: Under DBS v SRG, a retroactive reduction is available for a payor who had reduced income; the court applies the four DBS factors in reverse (delay, recipient's conduct, child's circumstances, hardship from retroactivity); Theo's four-year delay is a significant negative factor; courts may grant a partial retroactive reduction but are unlikely to grant the full four years given the length of the delay and the fact that April and the children relied on the higher support.
options.C: Retroactive reductions are never available; child support may only be varied prospectively.
options.D: A payor who finds new employment cannot seek any retroactive reduction for the period of lower income; the claim is barred.

explanation: In DBS v SRG, [2006] 2 SCR 231, the Supreme Court confirmed that retroactive relief can be sought by payors as well as recipients. The same four-factor framework applies: (1) the payor's reason for the delay in bringing the application; (2) the recipient's conduct (did April benefit from the higher support in a way that cannot be undone?); (3) the circumstances of the children; and (4) hardship to the recipient from a retroactive reduction. Theo's four-year delay is a significant negative factor — payors are expected to bring variation applications promptly when circumstances change. Courts may grant a partial retroactive reduction (e.g., beginning one year before the formal application) but are unlikely to grant the full four years given the detrimental reliance of the recipient and children on the support payments received.
why_A_wrong: Retroactive reductions are not automatic for any period of lower income. The DBS four-factor framework applies; a four-year delay is a strong negative factor against a full retroactive reduction.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. DBS applies to payor retroactive reduction claims; four-year delay is a major negative factor; courts may grant partial retroactive reduction; full four-year reduction unlikely given reliance on payments.
why_C_wrong: Retroactive reductions are available under the DBS framework; they are not limited to prospective variations. The question is the extent to which retroactive relief is appropriate given the four factors.
why_D_wrong: A payor's subsequent re-employment does not bar a retroactive reduction claim for the period of genuine reduced income. The DBS framework evaluates all circumstances; the payor's good faith and the period of actual reduced income are relevant factors.

source_reference.heading: Retroactive Child Support — DBS v SRG Payor Reduction Claim
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: DBS v SRG; LJW v TAR; Henry v Henry; Hiemstra v Hiemstra, [2006] 2 SCR 231

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Interjurisdictional Support Orders — Payor Moves to Different Province or Country
fact_pattern: Cheryl lives in Ontario with her two children and has a child support order requiring her former spouse Daniel to pay $1,400/month. Daniel has moved to British Columbia and claims he is no longer subject to the Ontario order. Cheryl's lawyer must advise her on how to enforce the Ontario order against Daniel in British Columbia.
call_of_question: How does Ontario's interjurisdictional support enforcement framework address the situation where a payor has moved to another province?

options.A: The Ontario order becomes unenforceable once Daniel leaves Ontario; Cheryl must obtain a new order from the BC courts.
options.B: Under Ontario's Interjurisdictional Support Orders Act, 2002 (ISO Act) and the corresponding BC legislation, a support order made in Ontario may be registered and enforced in BC without the need to re-litigate the support; the Family Responsibility Office (FRO) in Ontario may coordinate enforcement with the BC reciprocating authority.
options.C: Cheryl must personally travel to BC and commence a new child support proceeding there.
options.D: An Ontario support order is automatically void once the payor leaves Ontario; it has no legal effect in another province.

explanation: Under Interjurisdictional Support Orders Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 13, Ontario's Interjurisdictional Support Orders Act, 2002 (ISO Act), S.O. 2002, c. 13, provides a mechanism for registering and enforcing Ontario support orders in other Canadian provinces (and some foreign countries). Ontario has reciprocal enforcement arrangements with all other Canadian provinces. Cheryl can register the Ontario order in British Columbia through the ISO Act mechanism, which allows BC authorities to enforce the order as if it were a BC order. The Family Responsibility Office (FRO) in Ontario coordinates enforcement and can work with its BC counterpart. Daniel cannot escape the Ontario order merely by crossing a provincial border. No new BC proceeding or fresh child support determination is required for enforcement of the existing order.
why_A_wrong: An Ontario support order does not become unenforceable when the payor moves to another province. The ISO Act and reciprocal enforcement mechanisms ensure the order remains enforceable across provincial borders.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The ISO Act allows registration and enforcement of Ontario support orders in other provinces; FRO coordinates enforcement; no new proceeding is required for enforcement of the existing order.
why_C_wrong: Cheryl does not need to travel to BC or commence fresh proceedings. The ISO Act mechanism allows registration in BC through an administrative process coordinated by FRO and the BC authority.
why_D_wrong: Support orders made in one Canadian province are not void when the payor moves to another province. Reciprocal enforcement arrangements between provinces ensure cross-border enforceability of support orders.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Interjurisdictional Support — ISO Act
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Interjurisdictional Support Orders Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 13; Family Responsibility and Support Arrears Enforcement Act, 1996, S.O. 1996, c. 31
